summary: Cantrill recounts his experience running VENIX 2.0, a 1984 UNIX variant, on a DEC Pro 350 simulator. He marvels at how recognizable the basic tools remain after twenty years—vi, cc, and adb all feel familiar. Yet he's equally struck by how primitive the system is compared to modern operating systems, lacking TCP/IP, real virtual memory, and proper observability, which highlights how far systems software has advanced.
key_points: VENIX 2.0 ran easily on a modern Solaris 10 system via the Xhomer simulator
Core UNIX tools like vi, cc, and adb remain recognizable after two decades
The 1984 system lacks fundamental modern features: TCP/IP, virtual memory, multiprocessor support
The experience illustrates both UNIX's remarkable stability and its dramatic evolution
money_quotes: Using a twenty year old UNIX is a strange experience: I'm amazed at how little the most basic things have changed.
I was familiar with all of the tools necessary to write a program (vi), compile it (cc) and debug it (adb).

While it's amazing how familiar VENIX feels, I'm also stunned by how anemic its facilites are: it has no TCP/IP stack, no real filesystem, no multiple processor support, no resource management, no dynamic linking, no real virtual memory system, no observability and poor debuggability.
It reminds me how far we have come – even before we embarked on the far more radical technologies found in Solaris 10.
Seeing adb spit out a true Algol stack backtrace would be like sipping from the debugging fountain of youth.
key_insight: UNIX's core abstractions have remained remarkably stable for decades, but the underlying systems infrastructure has evolved dramatically, reminding us that familiar interfaces can mask profound technological progress.
